text: how to deal with the first big breakfast Mom has made in almost two months. Orange juice and bacon and scrambled eggs and buttered toast all sit in my stomach like a dysfunctional family. I’m walking off the urge to crawl back into bed and go to sleep. I walk past Browning Street, and keep going until I reach Twin Oaks, where I stop and stare at the entrance, wondering if anyone’s up for a game of air hockey, but my heart’s not in it. I stuff my hands in my jean pockets and walk a little farther until I see the basketball courts. It’s a beautiful day out, and some boys are playing a game of H.O.R.S.E. I step closer and stop at the chain link fence. I recognize a few faces from school, but they don’t look in my direction, and I don’t bother to wave. I hear one of them make a joke about another boy’s mother, and though my face wants to break into a smile, it doesn’t happen. It stays inside me, hidden but bothersome. An unscratched itch. I’m uncertain of the last time I smiled. The wave hits me out of nowhere. I truly do not see or feel any warning at all, but suddenly, I’m clutching the fence with one hand and projectile vomiting into the weeds and confetti of cigarette butts by my feet. The bits of egg and bacon are slimy with my stomach bile and the thought of how slick they must feel makes me vomit again. Tears blur my vision and I feel a line of spittle hanging from my bottom lip. I blink to clear my eyes, and watch the drool swing like a silver pendulum. The sum of my insides shake and quake and tremor. I spit into the weeds. My throat feels like I’ve spewed lava. I am a volcano. I let out a heavy sigh, use my hand to smear the spit from my lips, and then wipe my hand on my jeans. The game has come to a halt and I realize all of the boys are frozen on the court, staring at me. I cough, spit again, and head back home. My back feels hot from their eyes on me. I don’t tell my parents I threw up, but later, when they’re sleeping and the house is quiet, I creep downstairs, placing my steps against the sides of the treads so I don’t make them creak. I check beneath the kitchen sink and I can’t find the little blue bottle I saw Mom with months ago. I can’t specifically figure out why that bothers me so much, but lately I feel like my breath is slowly seeping out of me and rising to the surface of the pool water. It takes me a long while to go to sleep. # Mom wears this gray sweater jacket all the time like a cloak a statue might wear. It’s slightly too big for her, so she has the sleeve ends folded back twice. I tried it on once when she was napping. It’s stiff and coarse and uncomfortable even though she’s had it for a long time. I’m not sure why she wears it. It’s almost like it refuses to be broken in. It’s become a uniform of sorts, and when Mom comes in to bring me soup or check on me, I realize it’s not the bright white or patterned nurse scrubs, but in a way, the gray sweater is Mom’s nurse uniform. I’m lying down and I can’t help but glance at Della’s bed, untouched for months by anyone except me. Sometimes at night, I curl up on it, and even though the linens have been washed, if I squeeze Della’s pillow against my face, I can still smell her there. Faintly. It’s kind of like the whisper of a memory of what she smelled like, but it’s something. My stomach burns and my mind recites Shakespeare, that part about fire burn and cauldron bubble. I’ve lost track of how many times I’ve thrown up, but it’s enough that I’ve stayed home from school for three weeks. To date, I’ve gotten a total of three Get Well Soon cards. My mind swims with current like the wake of a mermaid’s tail. Della laughing with me. Dancing together. Doing stupid skits in our bedroom, or inventing our secret handshake. I think of the time she used a red make-up pencil to draw a pimple on her chin so I wouldn’t feel so bad about the real one I had. Della went to school like that, too. All of these things make me smile, and I realize how foreign that feeling has become. I was born six minutes and thirty-two seconds before Della. I know this because Mom says thirty-two seconds in labor feels like an hour. So technically I’m the older sister, but the thing is, I look up to Della. She has some quality I will never have, and that’s okay. Until now, we’ve been together, literally forever. Split from the same cell, sharing everything , right on back to the moment of conception. I love her. She loves me, too. I should have known. A part of me knew, but I guess I kept my eyes tightly closed rather than face something uncomfortable . I consider opening the new issue of Girls’ Life Mom put on the nightstand for me, but simply touching the glossy pages makes me think of how my vomit looked at the basketball court. Besides, flipping the pages and seeing all the fake smiles is something I don’t want to deal with. The bedroom ceiling is off-white with age, and the plaster is a pattern of half-circle swirls from some kind of brush. I try counting the rough crescent moons on the ceiling, but lose track after I hit forty-three. I start over and reach fifty-seven before I lose my spot again, and I give up. I hear
